content.full_text:
FPPC

JAN 2 9 95 AM '90

John Frederick
Harbor Commissioner, 3rd District
Humboldt Bay Harbor, Recreation and Conservation District
P.O. Box 1030
Eureka, CA 95502-1030


December 28, 1989


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807


Dear Sirs,

I am requesting an "Advice Letter" to clarify my position concerning
any conflict of interest.

I was elected Harbor Commissioner on November 7, 1989 and took office
on December 1, 1989.  I had worked as a temporary employee (biological
technician) for the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service, Fisheries Assistance
Office in Arcata, CA from July 1988 until September 23, 1989.  This
office deals almost exclusively with the Klamath River anadromous
fisheries.

Humboldt Bay can be divided into three bays, the southernmost bay is
called the South Bay is comprised almost entirely by the Humboldt Bay
National Wildlife Refuge which is administered by the U.S. Fish &
Wildlife Service.  The Wildlife Refuge's main area of concern is with
migratory waterfowl.

I will have the opportunity to give my opinion regarding the Wildlife
Refuge (hunting regulations, etc.) as well as be engaged in
negotiations concerning the sale or lease of tidelands to the Wildlife
Refuge.  Does this represent any conflict of interest?  I have been
told by council to the Harbor District that this could be construed as
such.  I don't believe so.  Please help clarify this issue.


Sincerely,


John Frederick

M 91-019

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 21, 1990

TO: Advice File

FROM: Kevin S. Braaten-Meen

SUBJECT: When a gift of gubernatorial inaugural ball tickets is
received.

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

QUESTIONS

If tickets to the gubernatorial inaugural ball are given to a
legislator at no cost to the legislator, when is the "gift" made?

Does it matter if the tickets are left at the will call window for
the legislator to pick up at the event?

ANSWERS

When the tickets are received by the legislator is the moment at
which the gift is made. Thus, if the legislator receives the
tickets, in person or by mail, before January 1, 1991, the
legislator will not run afoul of the new gift limit provisions.

If the legislator is told that tickets to the event may be picked
up at the will call window the day of the event, the legislator
receives the gift when s/he picks up the tickets. Since the event
will take place in 1991, the legislator must take the new gift
limit provisions of the Act into consideration before accepting
the tickets.

If tickets are left at the will call window for the legislator and
the legislator invites someone else to use the tickets instead,
the legislator receives a gift from the giver and the attendee
receives the gift from the legislator when the attendee picks up
the tickets.

This advice concerns the gift limit and reporting provisions of
the Act, not the conflict of interest provisions.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660
